data.title: 3 Dividend Stocks I'm Buying As Irrational Exuberance Takes Over
data.description:
Some people collect rare stamps, comic books, coins, or baseball cards. (Or, these days, hideous Labubu dolls.)

Me? I collect charts.

Every week, I show off the latest additions to my chart collection in these articles in an effort to understand and explain the economy, markets, and dividend investing. On top of that, for what it's worth, I also reveal and discuss my buy list. I don't expect (or want) anybody to follow my buys religiously, but I do hope to be a good source of ideas for fellow dividend growth investors.

Now that the sales pitch is out of the way ("what do I need to do to get you in a brand new Mazda today?"), let's see those charts.

Here's the agenda this week:

  • An attempt to explain the weird and conflicting inflation data.
  • Some compelling signals that irrational exuberance has taken hold in both the stock market and parts of the economy.
  • More conflicting data about the trajectory of the economy, wherein the labor market points to weakness but affluent consumer spending signals resilience.
  • Some comments on my slimmed down buy list.

Onward!

(In/De/Disin)Flation

Inflation readings have been weird lately.

For August, the producer price index unexpectedly declined, whereas the consumer price index rose more than expected.

Perhaps this indicates that on the business-to-business side of the economy, companies are "eating the tariffs" and allowing margin compression for the time being, whereas on the business-to-consumer side of the economy, companies are hiking prices whenever they are able to.

There's some evidence for this. For example, this helpful chart shows the percentage of items within the CPI that exhibited at least 3% price increases by month:

As you can see, the number of consumer items seeing significant price increases has jumped to nearly 60% of the total, the highest since the Fall of 2022.

Jeremy Schwartz of WisdomTree compiles a "real-time inflation" metric every month, substituting the lagged BLS housing data for market rent rates from the private sector. Back in 2021-2022, real-time inflation was significantly higher than the CPI reported, but from 2023 through the present, real-time inflation has been lower than the CPI reports.

This aligns almost perfectly with data from another private consumer price aggregator, Truflation, which currently (as of this writing) shows almost exactly the same level of inflation.

While non-lagged inflation is (and has been for years) lower than the reported number, it's notable that all inflation readings are heading higher. That is with residential rent rates basically flat in the face of a huge amount of new multifamily supply.

Used cars & trucks, tobacco & smoking products, and alcoholic beverages (all heavily exposed to imports and therefore tariffs) saw sharp price increases in August.

A lot of crosscurrents are intersecting right now to cast the inflation picture into disarray. A financially weak paycheck-to-paycheck (low- to moderate-income) consumer, a resilient affluent consumer, ample corporate margin buffer to temporarily absorb tariffs, and uncertainty about the enduring legality of across-the-board tariffs are keeping the inflation outlook uncertain.

The only certainty is that if tariffs remain in place indefinitely, their cost will be borne mostly by American businesses and consumers.

Irrational Exuberance

Irrational exuberance is hard to measure or define, but you know it when you see it.

It isn't just about asset valuations. It's a broad, culture-wide attitude toward risk. It is when a society seems to forget that bad things can happen and instead becomes consumed by the fear of missing out. It is the sense that others out there are getting rich quick, and I need to get rich quick as well. It is the sense that if I don't participate in the momentum right now, I will miss out and forever regret it.

Given the incredible and market-beating rallies in silver (SLV), gold (GLD), and Bitcoin this year, it is striking to find this exuberance in both traditionally "risk-on" assets and "risk-off" assets.

The S&P 500 (SPY) has returned a cool 12%+ this year, which is certainly respectable. Stocks are participating in the exuberance, to be sure. But the most exciting action is arguably happening outside the stock market, including in precious metals and crypto.

Bitcoin's rally appears to be driven primarily by a risk-on attitude, whereas gold and silver are probably driven by a combination of high fiscal deficits, geopolitical friction, a weakening US dollar, and the perception among many that stocks are expensive.

But the non-stock exuberance doesn't end there. No, no! We haven't even gotten to the most exciting stuff.

Like Pokemon cards, which are trouncing the SPY and even beating many AI-related stocks like Meta Platforms (META) this year.

The last time Pokemon cards surged in price like this was in 2020 and early 2021, during the peak of the bout of irrational exuberance. META also reached a peak during this period. Baseball card prices peaked a bit later, around the beginning of 2022.

Even the current Labubu doll craze seems reminiscent of the late '90s Beanie Baby bubble. Some have pointed out the striking similarities between the two phenomena. Collectors are frenetically accumulating dolls and driving up resale prices.

In 1998, when I was 7 years old, I went to the MLB All-Star baseball game in Denver, where everyone received a special edition Beanie Baby named "Glory." Outside the stadium after the game, people ran around, offering to buy these Beanie Babies for $300, $400, $500 or more. My dad and brother sold theirs, but I refused to part with mine.

("Let your winners ride" was my 7-year-old self's motto, you might say.)

Hopefully it isn't lost on you, dear reader, that the Beanie Baby bubble coincided with the Dot Com bubble of the late 1990s. On the surface, the two appear unrelated, but one could argue that they were both part of the broader zeitgeist of exuberance.

If the mad frenzy for precious metals, or for crypto, or for Pokemon cards, or for Labubu dolls was happening in isolation, it might simply be an interesting phenomenon. But when all are happening simultaneously, it strikes me as strong evidence that irrational exuberance has taken hold.

Now add to that high stock valuations.

The SPY's forward P/E ratio of over 22x has only been this high (in the modern era) in 2021 and in the late 1990s.

While the late '90s were clearly recognized as a bubble, both periods could accurately be characterized as being in the grips of irrational exuberance.

Notice in the chart below on the right that it isn't only the largest 10 stocks trading at rich valuations. The 490 stocks below them in the SPY are also trading at around the same level as their peak valuations in the late 1990s.

Then again, you'll notice that today, unlike the period from 1996 to 2000, the market cap and earnings of the largest US companies are growing roughly in line with each other. Back in the Dot Com bubble era, the market cap of Internet-related tech stocks surged first, and only in the following years (2001-2002) did the largest companies' earnings follow suit.

It isn't that every detail of today's tech-driven market is the same as it was during the Dot Com bubble. It's that the level of investor euphoria, risk appetite, and irrational exuberance today is reminiscent of the way it was back then.

It's the zeitgeist. It's in the social water we drink and the social air we breathe.

This doesn't necessarily mean the market is going to crash tomorrow, or next month, or perhaps ever. It's just a mere observation.

We are living in a period of irrational exuberance.

Economic Update: Weak Labor Market, Resilient Consumer

Paradoxically, the aforementioned irrational exuberance currently coexists with a rather mediocre and stagnant economy.

Fueled by flat to negative net immigration, AI-driven productivity increases, and the end of the post-COVID stimulus boom, growth in total US jobs has slowed to under 1%.

Historically, job growth slowing to less than 1% has been a pretty reliable indicator of an oncoming recession, but in the past, pre-recession slowdowns in job growth have been sharper than the gradual and grinding slowdown over the past few years.

For what it's worth, sentiment among those in the labor force today has sharply soured in recent months. Workers believe that getting a new job in a reasonable period of time would be very difficult today, and expectations of layoffs have been creeping higher over the last few years.

Sentiment about one's ability to obtain another job within 3 months if laid off has collapsed to its lowest level since the Great Financial Crisis. Is that just the fear of AI? Or does it communicate something about the economy more broadly?

Well, it is notable that after the brief temp worker boom in 2021 and early 2022, the number of temporary workers has slid rapidly.

Back in normal times, such huge and rapid declines in the temp workforce would reliably signal a weakening economy and an oncoming recession. Is it different this time?

Well, one thing is definitely different: The temp workforce has rapidly declined for three consecutive years without an officially declared recession. The economy continues to hum along just fine with fewer and fewer temp workers.

My guess is that AI will add fuel to this fire as employers find ways to make their hired staff more productive rather than utilize temp workers.

But while the labor market looks rather grim, other parts of the economy appear much more cheery.

For example, retail sales continue to grow.

Although real (inflation-adjusted) retail sales growth is rather anemic at less than 1%, it is still positive, which is better than most of the 2022-2023 period could say.

Affluent consumers continue to retain their jobs and continue to spend money. And the most recent wage growth data from Bank of America indicates that after a multi-year hiatus after the pandemic, higher-income households once again enjoy accelerating wage growth.

In the years following COVID, when the US suffered a severe labor shortage, lower-income households enjoyed rapid wage growth. Today, however, lower-income workers are seeing smaller and smaller raises, while higher-income workers are earning more and more — perhaps as a result of productivity gains.

The K-shaped economy reigns supreme.

This matters because affluent consumers account for most of the country's consumption. As of 2023, the top 40% of earners drove 60% of total consumer spending.

More recent data from Moody's Analytics suggests that the top 10% of earning households do roughly half of all consumer spending in the US.

If the affluent are doing well, then the US economy should also be doing well.

Here's one more positive sign: growth in bank lending is on the rise.

Bank lending growth isn't necessarily a leading indicator of recession, though. Prior to the 1990-1991 and 2001 recessions, growth in bank lending did slide considerably. But in the 2008-2009 recession — the worst since the Great Depression — bank lending didn't take a hit until well into the recession.

In any case, rising growth in bank lending probably can't be interpreted as a negative for the economy.

My Buy List

How then shall we invest amid all these confusing signals?

In a word: cautiously.

"Be fearful when others are greedy," as the Oracle of Omaha once quipped.

Your humble scribe has chosen the dividend growth investor's life, and my buy list reflects that. Assuming prices remain about where they are today, here's where I plan to devote any investable cash over this week:

Stocks Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 3.5% Mid-Single-Digit
American Tower (AMT) 3.5% Mid- to High-Single-Digit
H2O America (HTO) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit
ETFs
iShares Ultra Short Duration Bond ETF (ICSH) 4.5% N/A
SPDR Utilities ETF (XLU) 2.6% Mid- to High-Single-Digit

Since I discussed AMT and XLU last week and have nothing new to add about HTO, I'll focus my comments elsewhere.

AMH is the only vertically integrated single-family rental REIT, meaning that it operates an internal build-to-rent development platform alongside its massive, largely Sunbelt and Midwestern single-family rental portfolio.

A weakening labor market isn't great for AMH, but the good news is that demographics and home buying affordability are still very favorable to SFR landlords like AMH.

Moreover, AMH looks reasonably valued. Its dividend yield of 3.5% is the highest it's ever been, its AFFO multiple of ~20x is around its lowest level in the last five years, and its price to operating cash flow is about 10% below its long-term average.

A very large cohort of Millennials are entering their prime single-family living years of life without the ability to afford buying a single-family home. That will undoubtedly channel a lot of that demand into the SFR market.

A near-future recession would certainly justify the price decline in AMH, to be sure. But (1) a near-future recession is far from guaranteed, and (2) AMH's long-term outlook remains strong, in my view.

Back to Partial Cash-Raising Mode

Given the above discussions about irrational exuberance and a weakening labor market, I deem it prudent to return to incremental cash-raising. My favorite vehicle to act as a cash equivalent is ICSH, both because of its low expense ratio of 8 basis points and its low-volatility.

Notice that the downside ICSH experienced during the rate-hiking cycle of 2022 was fairly minimal relative to peers like JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST), PGIM Ultra Short Bond ETF (PULS), and Vanguard Ultra-Short Bond ETF (VUSB):

For these reasons, I view ICSH as arguably the best place to stash cash.

While I'm not predicting it, if there is a significant correction in the stock market anytime soon, I want to have more dry powder than I presently do to take advantage of it.

data.title: Imperial Oil: We Like It, But Think It's A Little Over Its Skis
data.description:
Introduction

Imperial Oil Limited (NYSE:IMO) reported mixed Q2 results in early August, and investors seem to have looked past that and bid the shares about 10% higher since. A nice beat on EPS (-$0.18) was followed by a substantial miss on Revenues (-$529m), which we can ascribe to lower realizations. As noted in the comparison graph below, IMO has outperformed its peer group substantially over the last six months and now trades at 15X EPS.

Curiously, other members of this group, Suncor Energy (SU) and Cenovus Energy (CVE), also are fetching a 15X, despite either leading the group—CVE with a 28% price return or bringing up the rear—SU with a 13% return. CNQ is an also-ran with less than a 6% return over the last six months.

The upward trend for the heavy oil producers certainly stands in contrast with the performance of some U.S. shale powerhouses. All of which are solid companies, generating free cash and shareholder returns. It doesn't seem to matter right now, as the oil price macro is so weak.

IMO has ratcheted its way higher to a near 9X gain over the last five years, making those who bought at the bottom pretty happy. The question was asked in the call about what might remain in the tank for continued outperformance of this type:

Analyst, Manav Gupta- When we look at year-to-date relative outperformance, you have strongly outperformed your peers. You're an absolute and a relative winner. Why does management feel the setup is still very strong for our investment case for Imperial, where it could continue to work on an absolute and a relative basis?

CEO John Whelan- our strategy is all about responsibly increasing cash flow and delivering unmatched industry-leading shareholder returns. I believe we have competitive advantages that, when matched up against those assets that we have, take our unique competitive advantages around scale and integration, technology execution, and the best team in the business.

Analyst, Manav Gupta- And just for the record, we completely agree with you. We believe the setup is very strong for your stock to continue to work.

I would say that results speak for themselves, and as we go through the other details in the Q-2 filings, we are prone to giving IMO the benefit of the doubt.

The thesis for IMO

IMO, it is in the club of sand miners with billions of barrels of P2 heavy oil reserves that provide a long runway of future revenues. The company has largely grown its production organically through investment in its core operating bases at Kearl, Syncrude, and Cold Lake. It has avoided the high-cost M&A that other companies have found necessary to scale up their production, with no acquisitions since 2004.

As a result, at least in part, its balance sheet is relatively pristine, with only $2.2b in debt and a share float of only 500m. A D/E ratio of 16% is about half that of other competitors in the space. IMO is a technology company with strong ties to ExxonMobil (XOM) through its ~70% ownership of IMO. The company is also Canada's largest refiner, with plants at Strathcona and Sarnia and a newly inaugurated 20K BOPD Renewable Diesel plant within the Strathcona complex. IMO also has retail distribution through 2,600 Esso and Mobil fueling and service stations.

IMO forecasts a decline in sustaining and overall capex from 2026 and a higher proportion of total capex going to growth projects, enabling the increase in output through 2029. IMO, it lists cash costs below $20 per bbl at Kearl and Cold Lake, enabling profitable operations at low WTI prices.

Finally, IMO has very shareholder-friendly capital allocation policies that have both reduced the share count by 39% since 2018. This has enabled a ten-year CAGR dividend growth rate in the high teens and accelerating in the last five years to the low 20s.

A future catalyst for IMO

The pilot of the Enhanced Bitumen Recovery Technology (ERBT) provides a lever for squeezing more production from current assets at a lower cost. This is done through solvent-assisted steam injection that lowers the amount of steam-and therefore the energy required to produce it—over other SAGD projects. 2027 is not as far off as it used to be, and as the slide below illustrates, ERBT could bring substantial new production if it lives up to its potential.

Using solvents to enhance production is nothing new. Solvents of various types are routinely used in completions and production scenarios to break down reservoir gunk and ease (lower differential pressures) the path of hydrocarbons into the wellbore. Here's the secret.

Oil formations are made of porous material-clastics, that provide void space-pores, for liquids to accumulate over time. By the nature of the sedimentation that created the reservoir, these pores are of varying sizes. It takes more energy to evacuate a small pore than a larger pore. By incorporating solvent with the steam, another level to enable a pore to permit hydrocarbon flow, you add the power of chemistry to thermal energy, and get more production. That's the theory anyway. Now all they have to do is show it will generate an IRR consistent with corporate objectives, and they are off to the races.

Obviously, another catalyst is its shareholder-friendly attitude toward capital allocation. Doug Leggate, an analyst for Wolfe Research, posed a question about further enhancements to the dividend:

What we think drives market recognition of value for your stock is dividend growth. So my question is that you have the lowest dividend breakeven still in the industry, not just in Canada. But your dividend growth could go a lot higher, if you like, or a lot higher—your absolute dividend could go a lot higher without having to rely on the buybacks to manage the burden because your breakeven is so low. So my question is, why not?

John Whelan, CEO:

We do prioritize a reliable and growing dividend. That is our first priority in terms of returning cash to our shareholders. So it is still our first priority in terms of returning cash to shareholders. But we're also comfortable with the mix here in terms of the dividends versus share buybacks. And as we've talked to investors, the approach we've taken on share buybacks continues to get very positive feedback.

It seems that in the event—we aren't holding our breath—where crude prices rise substantially, there would be an opportunity to goose the dividend, and perhaps investors could also see special dividends.

Q2 2025

IMO, posted net income for the quarter was $949m, a decline from Q-1 of over $300m due to lower realizations. Operating cash flow was also down QoQ $1,465m compared to $1,527m in Q-1. Their total upstream production of 427,000 BOPD outperformed for the quarter.

During the quarter, Imperial returned $367 million to shareholders through dividend payments and declared a third-quarter dividend of 72 cents per share. IMO also renewed its annual normal course issuer bid to repurchase up to five percent of outstanding common shares. They expect to complete purchases prior to year-end 2025.

Guidance from the company for full-year production of 433-456K BOPD incorporates growth of about 5% YoY in the upper end of this range.

Company filings.

Risks

A couple of things come to mind that can impact forecasts for IMO. The first is the WCS discount. Some of this risk has been tempered by the opening up of the TMX export line to the west. There is nothing like new takeaway capacity to keep that discount in check. The second is the reference price for WTI (CL1:COM). Also known as crude oil, prices for this commodity have been under pressure this year, with the NYMEX contract falling from the mid-$70s to the low-$60s since President Trump's inauguration.

Another risk to the stock is just how far it's run ahead of competitors. All of these companies are technology-oriented and looking for ways to enhance production from their assets. We could certainly see a reversion to its 200-day SMA in the $72-$75 range. It should be noted that the Q3 EPS forecast of $1.40 is about a 15% bump from Q2 and a little weaker in Q4 at $1.26. Price targets range from $58 to $95, suggesting that IMO could see some downside pressure if EPS forecasts are not met.

Your takeaway

Analysts have a hold rating on the stock due to valuation. Let's face it, IMO has run like a thoroughbred with a 50% gain just since early April. The stock is trading at nearly 8X EV/EBITDA and a price per flowing barrel of $101. Both of these are pretty rich valuations, certainly in the present oil price dynamic. In my view, the growth we can expect for 2025 is priced into the stock at current levels.

Acknowledging the market's preference for IMO, thanks to dividend growth, I still think the current state of affairs creates enough risk that I don't want to buy in at the current prices. I will wait for some sort of pullback, which we might see as we exit the year. If crude prices nudge their way into the $50s, as is widely suspected they will, a 10-15% better entry point might just present itself. Accordingly, IMO gets a Hold rating at current levels.

data.title: Agree Realty: Now One Of The Best REITs On Wall Street
data.description:
Readers who have followed my work know that I'm committed to the fundamentals of the companies that I own/cover. That's because, when the dust settles, fundamentals are what drive total returns.

This is why positive developments with a company's fundamentals warm my heart as a dividend growth investor. This can include encouraging news from credit rating agencies, raised guidance, and dividend boosts.

That brings me to my subject of today, which is Agree Realty (NYSE:ADC). When I last covered it with a buy rating in July, I thought it was set for the next leg of growth. I liked the recent additions to ADC's team and the additional liquidity as well. Finally, the stock looked like a decent value.

Two months later, I'm sticking with my buy rating. That's because Fitch issued an A- credit rating to ADC last month. The net lease REIT also upped its guidance for 2025 in its Q2 report in July. I also believe a 1.6% raise in the monthly dividend per share to $0.26 is around the corner (ADC raises its dividend each April and October). Lastly, shares are almost as undervalued as they were in July.

Agree Realty Is Officially An A-Rated REIT

On July 31st, ADC released its financial results for the second quarter ended June 30th, 2025. The net lease REIT's total revenue rose by 15% over the year-ago period to $175.5 million during the quarter. That beat Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter by roughly $2.5 million.

This absolute topline growth easily outpaced the 9.9% uptick in the weighted average diluted share count for the quarter to 110.4 million shares. Thus, revenue growth on a per-share basis came in right around 5% during the quarter.

What led to ADC's surge in revenue in the second quarter? Like in previous articles, this was largely driven by its acquisitions in recent quarters. That caused the company's total property count to grow by 14.1% year-over-year to 2,513 to end Q2 2025.

In Q2 2025, ADC invested more than $350 million in 110 properties, according to President and CEO Joey Agree's opening remarks during the Q2 2025 Earnings Call. Noteworthy acquisitions included a Walmart Supercenter in Ohio, a sale-leaseback with a leading national auto parts retailer, and a $75 million grocery-dominated portfolio.

The latter was one of its largest non-sale-leaseback transactions since the inception of the company's acquisition platform in 2010. This deal was 18 months in the making, working on the off-market opportunity.

Overall, the deals closed in Q2 2025 were done so at a 7.1% weighted average cap rate with a weighted average lease term of 12.2 years. More than half (53%) of the base rent acquired came from investment-grade retailers.

Compared to a weighted average cost of capital of 5.8%, these deals were completed with a 130 basis point spread.

Contractual rent increases were the secondary contributor to revenue growth for ADC for the second quarter. The net lease REIT's occupancy also remained strong at 99.6% to close out the quarter.

ADC's AFFO per share grew by 1.9% over the year-ago period to $1.06 during the second quarter. Higher financing costs led the net lease REIT's AFFO margin to contract by around 200 basis points to 67% in the quarter.

Moving forward, ADC's AFFO per share growth should accelerate. On its face, this would seem contradictory.

AFFO per share through the first half of 2025 was only up 2.4% to $2.13, right? Yes, but not so fast.

The net lease REIT was confident enough to raise AFFO per share guidance for 2025. This was upped from the previous midpoint of $4.285 ($4.27 to $4.30) to $4.305 ($4.29 to $4.32). The new guidance would be a 4% growth rate over 2024 at the midpoint.

Why is ADC optimistic? Well, Joey Agree noted that the net lease REIT is coming out of just over $900 million in 2024 investment volume - its lowest level since 2019. That's why AFFO per share growth began the year at a rather muted level.

It's important to emphasize that ADC is seeing more attractive opportunities for acquisitions now. This is supported by the fact that the company upped its 2025 acquisitions guidance again from a midpoint of $1.4 billion ($1.3 billion to $1.5 billion) to $1.5 billion ($1.4 billion to $1.6 billion).

As I alluded to earlier, the investment spread on acquisitions in Q2 2025 was around 130 basis points. This is solidly in the 100 to 150 basis points range that ADC argues generates healthy accretion.

Better yet, the company's sandbox of 170,000 net lease opportunities with best-in-class retailers provides it with a long growth runway.

That's why I believe the FAST Graphs analyst consensus for ADC is realistic. In 2025, the consensus is for AFFO per share to grow by 4% to $4.31. Another 5.2% jump in AFFO per share to $4.53 is anticipated for 2026. In 2027, an additional 4.8% increase in AFFO per share to $4.75 is the current consensus.

Financially, ADC remains in an enviable position. The company had more than $2.3 billion in total liquidity, no meaningful debt maturities until 2028, and a pro forma net debt to recurring EBITDA ratio of 3.1x at quarter end. This robust financial condition recently translated into Fitch issuing an A- credit rating to ADC. That makes the net lease REIT one of only 13 publicly listed REITs with an A- or better credit rating equivalent. S&P and Moody's credit ratings aren't lacking, either, with the REIT enjoying BBB+ equivalent ratings from each rating agency (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to ADC's Q2 2025 Earnings Press Release, ADC's Q2 2024 Earnings Press Release, and ADC's August 2025 Investor Presentation).

Double-Digit Upside To Fair Value

In the two months since my prior article, ADC has posted a 4% total return. Once again, that's in the ballpark of the S&P 500 index's (SP500) posted during that time.

Even after this decent appreciation in recent months, I contend that the net lease REIT's shares are still a reasonable deal. ADC's stock is currently priced at a forward 12-month P/AFFO ratio of 16.4. For additional context, the stock's seven-year average P/AFFO ratio is 19.4, according to FAST Graphs.

As I detailed in July, I believe this is the more appropriate time frame to value ADC. This is because the net lease REIT has improved its tenant quality and secured credit rating upgrades from rating agencies in recent years.

Overall, I'm reiterating my fair value P/AFFO ratio of 18. That reflects the company's strengthened portfolio and intact growth profile.

My fair value multiple also accounts for the elevated 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y). The 4% yield remains well above the seven-year average yield of 2 3/4%, per Macrotrends. I also expect the 10-year yield to stay around the high 3% range moving forward.

Using my same estimate of a nearly 200 basis point spread between ADC's AFFO yield and the 10-year yield, this produces my fair value P/AFFO ratio of 18.

As of now, the calendar year 2025 is 70% complete. That suggests another 30% of 2025 and 70% of 2026 still lie ahead in the coming 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month AFFO per share input of $4.46.

Using my fair value multiple of 18, I arrive at a fair value of $80 a share. Relative to the current $73 share price (as of September 11th, 2025), that works out to be a 9% discount to fair value.

If ADC reverts to fair value and matches the growth consensus, it could have a 17% upside through September 2026. By the end of 2030, ADC could post 10% annual total returns.

Expect Dividend Growth To Accelerate

ADC offers a 4.3% forward dividend yield. For more perspective, this remains about 40 basis points below the real estate sector's median forward yield of 4.7% (an improvement from a 50 basis point spread in July). As a result, the net lease REIT earns respective C and C- grades from Seeking Alpha's Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield (unchanged from July).

ADC's pedestrian yield versus peers is made up for by a variety of other areas in which the net lease REIT shines.

For instance, the company has raised its dividends for 13 years (adjusted for the decision to convert to a monthly dividend in 2021). That stands solidly above the sector median of 1.2 years and is sufficient for the Quant System to award an A grade for overall dividend consistency (unchanged from July).

ADC's dividend also remains very viable. The company's AFFO payout ratio is expected to be in the low 70% range in 2025. Per The Dividend Kings' Zen Research Terminal, that would be meaningfully better than the 90% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see for the industry.

According to CFO Peter Coughenour, this has the company on track to generate $120 million in free cash flow after the dividend in 2025. That would be up more than 15% from 2024. These elements are why the Quant System awards a B grade for overall dividend safety (up from a B- in July).

Lastly, ADC looks positioned for more dividend growth in the future. The Quant System anticipates 2.8% annual forward dividend per share growth. This would be better than the sector median forecast of 2.2%.

In my view, this is arguably too conservative. The company's likely to deliver 4% to 5% annual AFFO per share growth in the coming years. Couple that with the low payout ratio, and I envision dividend growth between 4% and 5% annually. This would be comparable to the 5 and 10-year annual dividend growth rates of 5.4%. That's enough to warrant a B+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend growth (unchanged from July).

Risks To Consider

From a fundamentals perspective, ADC is a promising net lease REIT. Even so, there are risks that could undermine the investment thesis if they materialize.

As was the case in July, geographic concentration remains a key risk to ADC. As of June 30th, 2025, 51.5% of the net lease REIT's annualized base rent was generated in the following markets (ADC's Q2 2025 Earnings Press Release):

  1. Texas (7.2%)
  2. Illinois (6.0%)
  3. Michigan (5.4%)
  4. Ohio (5.1%)
  5. Pennsylvania (5.0%)
  6. Florida (4.9%)
  7. New York (4.9%)
  8. North Carolina (4.8%)
  9. California (4.3%)
  10. Georgia (3.9%)

This is important to note because of a finding in my Visa (V) article from earlier this month. Datasets recently analyzed by Moody's chief economist Mark Zandi and his team found that states comprising about one-third of U.S. GDP are either in a recession or at a high risk of falling into one.

In the case of ADC, Illinois and Georgia already fall into this category. Other states like Michigan, Ohio, New York, and California are currently treading water.

Fortunately, ADC does mitigate this risk with nearly 70% of its tenants being investment-grade. Still, it makes sense to monitor this risk in the future.

A similar risk to ADC is the potential for especially severe natural disasters concentrated in its top markets. If this happens, the damage could exceed commercial insurance coverage in a worst-case scenario. Such an event could impair the company's capacity to generate AFFO.

Summary: Shares Remain A Buy

ADC continues to solidify its position as a best-in-breed REIT. The A- credit rating from Fitch is the latest example that lends credence to this argument. The company also has the investment sandbox to sustain acquisitions and AFFO per share growth for many more years. For my money, shares are discounted, too. Consequently, I'm reiterating my buy rating.

data.title: I'm Convinced Energy Is The Most Misunderstood Investment Opportunity Of The Decade
data.description:
Introduction

In the first months of this year, I frequently discussed defense contractors. I brought them up so much that it started to annoy me a tiny bit.

However, it was worth it.

As many of my readers have found out in recent years, whenever I find an industry or sector with tremendous value, I dedicate a lot of time and energy to it. Given my long-term investment horizon, I concentrate on big trends, which means my preferred investment topics tend to receive attention for a long period, until they are no longer "table-pounding" buys, one could say.

Earlier this year, defense contractors were extremely cheap, as the market was worried about disruption risks, government funding uncertainty (remember the DOGE Department?), and because value stocks, in general, were out of favor.

That didn't last.

  • Disruptors like SpaceX, Anduril, and Palantir (PLTR) are doing well, yet they are far away from making existing players obsolete.
  • The market has figured out that DOGE isn't a major risk anymore, as government spending is running hot and defense is getting a lot of love from the current president.
  • European defense spending is tremendously benefiting the Americans, as they are leaders with major exposure in every single defense niche.

As a result, of the major defense contractors that I cover, only Lockheed Martin (LMT) has failed to beat the market on a year-to-date basis, mainly due to issues that are unrelated to the "Big Picture." Companies like Northrop Grumman (NOC), L3Harris Technologies (LHX), and RTX Corp. (RTX) are in high demand, as they have proven to be more than able to deliver next-gen technologies, win big orders, and generate tremendous shareholder value.

With that said, I'm not bringing this up as a victory lap. After all, I am not selling any of my defense stocks. I am explaining how it often takes time for a thesis to unfold. Without patience, I can honestly say, I would not have captured any of the big gains I have made in recent years. None of them.

Needless to say, this investment style isn't for anyone. Investors who simply buy index funds on a regular basis shouldn't try to time the market or develop specific theses. For them, making money often relies on these three rules:

  • Consistency over timing. Investing regularly, even during periods of uncertainty, can help reduce the emotional pressure of trying to pick the “perfect” moment.
  • Your timeline shapes your perspective. The longer your time horizon, the less any single market entry point tends to influence long-term outcomes.
  • Diversification spreads exposure. A mix of assets doesn't eliminate risk, but it can help manage it by reducing reliance on any one investment or market movement.

However, as this is Seeking Alpha, most of us maintain an approach that is at least a little bit active, which is why it makes so much sense to spend time on finding undervalued opportunities, especially because the market is not cheap.

With that said, this isn't about defense. This is about another group of stocks I am super bullish on, a sector the market seems to "hate" even more than defense contractors earlier this year.

That sector is energy, a group of stocks that absolutely drives me nuts due to its cyclical behavior, volatility, headline-dependence, and poor returns for more than three years. Honestly, I absolutely get it when people say they cannot stand energy stocks.

Over the past three years, energy stocks have returned just 23%, using the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE) as a benchmark. While that's not necessarily a reason to "hate" the industry, it has caused tremendous frustration, as a simple investment in the S&P 500 would have returned 70% during this period. Moreover, energy investors were prone to way more volatility from factors like OPEC headlines and poor cyclical growth.

The good news is that, as much as I have started to dislike reading energy headlines every day and dealing with the volatility, I absolutely love the value this sector brings to the table, which is the number one reason I discuss this topic so often.

  • Energy is one of the cheapest sectors of the S&P 500 and a key bottleneck in the massive AI revolution, as I wrote in a recent article titled "Oh Boy, The Entire Stock Market Hinges On Just 1% Of Stocks."
  • With regard to the bullet point above, Goldman Sachs data confirms the valuation part, as energy is the cheapest sector on a 10-year basis. It would be even cheaper if we were to assume oil prices were to rally again.

  • The sector has something for every type of investor, including low-yield growers, high-yield oil drillers, ultra-high-margin landowners, high-yield midstream, and so much more.
  • In an environment of rising inflation, energy is perfect, as it has, historically speaking, the highest level of outperformance over inflation and the highest probability to beat inflation (see below).

In this article, I'll update my thesis, using new developments that could indicate we're buying energy at bottom prices right now.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get right to it!

Peak Negativity Is Here

Technically speaking, there's just one chart that matters when it comes to investing in oil and related equities. That chart is the one below, which shows the comparison between the leading ISM Manufacturing Index and the year-over-year change of WTI crude oil prices (CL1:COM).

Unfortunately, the pandemic has made it impossible to overlay the two lines, as the massive price decline followed by a steep rally has created a huge outlier situation.

Nonetheless, we clearly see a pattern, as higher growth expectations have always caused oil to rise. Meanwhile, declining expectations have pressured the world's most important energy commodity. To make matters worse, the ISM Index has been below 50 (the neutral line) almost nonstop since 2022.

On top of these issues, the biggest oil producers in the world, OPEC, decided it was a great idea to hike output. Usually, OPEC cuts output whenever prices are falling. Now, they are doing the opposite.

The most recent development was revealed on September 6, when the cartel announced another rapid unwind of past production cuts:

Key alliance members said they expect to approve adding about 137,000 barrels during a video call on Sunday, as the group led by Saudi Arabia and Russia begins unwinding the next layer of halted supplies, having just completed the surprise fast-tracking of a previous tranche. An October increase will start the return of 1.66 million barrels a day of cuts that were scheduled to remain in place until the end of 2026. Some of the delegates added that discussions are ongoing. - Bloomberg.

And to be completely honest, "nobody" really knows what OPEC is doing here, not even the oil experts I asked, who trade hundreds of millions worth of oil for large customers. They all have theories but limited intel.

Generally speaking, not knowing why the biggest producers were boosting output, weak cyclical growth, and the fact that there was a lot more money to be made in other areas (think of AI hyperscalers) caused energy stocks to massively underperform, as the ratio between the SPDR S&P Oil & Gas Exploration & Production ETF (XOP) to the S&P 500 erased almost its entire post-pandemic gains, as the chart below visualizes quite clearly.

This also caused bullish oil bets to fall to a 16-year low.

Needless to say, all of this is bad.

However, I believe it's a clear case of "peak negativity," as bad news is slowly fading, likely resulting in the market uncovering a highly favorable risk/reward.

I believe this is confirmed by the fact that oil actually rose after the latest oil supply boost announcement:

Allow me to elaborate on that.

The Energy Bull Case Is Looking Good

As annoying as OPEC's output hikes may have been, they have created a new bullish thesis, for at least two major reasons:

  • A large part of the production hike will not be felt by the market, as "some members of the group face pressure to compensate for earlier oversupply and forgo their share of production hikes," as Bloomberg wrote in the article I just quoted.
  • Most nations have run out of spare capacity, which now leaves the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia as the most powerful player in the group. Again, while nobody really knows what goes on behind closed doors, I believe recent moves may have been a way for Saudi Arabia to regain full pricing power over the group before oil moves higher. If that were the goal, it would certainly have been achieved.

That's why the next quote from the same article is so important, as it essentially shows two things, which are that oil markets are way tighter than one might expect and that OPEC has now gotten rid of the safety capacity to potentially increase output in case oil prices rise too fast in the future.

If ratified, the group’s decision to start unwinding its next layer of cuts also reflects a tension that has dominated oil markets for months: forecasters are issuing mounting warnings about a looming supply surplus, and yet markets have remained relatively tight over the northern hemisphere summer.

For global oil markets in the longer term, the OPEC+ move serves to erode a longstanding safety net of idle production that could be brought back to cushion unforeseen supply shocks. - Bloomberg.

This matters a lot, as the Middle East is, by far, the most important export market, as we can see below. Any tightness in that market will be felt around the globe, especially because oil production growth has basically flatlined in North America, which was the number one growth engine since 2009.

We also need to keep in mind that investments in oil and gas production have been lagging behind in recent years, as subdued prices were simply too much of an issue for companies to commit to production growth. That's a problem, as we will see a massive oil supply decline if new capital does not start flowing into new oil and gas fields, as the data below shows.

Needless to say, I believe there is no way the oil supply actually falls. However, that is because any supply headwinds will cause prices to increase, making it more attractive for producers to invest in CapEx. That's bullish for oil, as an incentive is required for that to happen. At $65 WTI (or below), that's unlikely to happen, as American shale requires prices of $70-$75 for sustainable investments, while Saudi Arabia requires $90 Brent to be fiscally breakeven.

In this case, fiscally breakeven means the nation requires elevated oil prices to balance its budget, including massive growth plans to transform and modernize its economy. That's another reason why I cannot imagine these OPEC price wars continuing to last.

Energy expert Eric Nuttall, whom I tremendously respect, shares this view, as he recently had two fascinating posts on X (formerly known as Twitter) that hit the nail on the head:

Is the oil market tighter than consensus believes? Chronic underperformance vs. "allowable production" would suggest so. The result: less spare capacity, tighter forward balances, less draconian (<$50/bbl) implied pricing, and Saudi 100% in the driver's seat going forward. - Eric Nuttall.

Moreover:

An oil market without spare capacity is like a home owner without fire insurance. This is a notable pivot by Saudi, historically the industry's insurance monopoly. What is taken for granted and unappreciated (cost of maintenance) can easily be taken away, as we are seeing now.

Adding to that, as I already briefly mentioned, I believe the market will figure out just how important energy is to fuel the AI revolution, on top of the demand benefits that come with a potential increase in cyclical growth.

That's why Jordi Visser, whom I have brought up in prior articles, made the case for a regime shift one cannot ignore, as there is an "insatiable demand for power," and a potential long power/short software trade, which could look something like being long Chevron (CVX) and short Salesforce (CRM).

Essentially, it would be the exact opposite of what happened between 2009 and 2021, when capital went into software. Moreover, hedge funds often shorted energy stocks while putting the proceeds into software.

Please note that I am not advocating that we need to start shorting software. I included this example from Mr. Visser because this is the kind of regime shift that AI could trigger in the future.

I am advocating for energy exposure to add inflation protection because I think power is the biggest AI bottleneck, because it's cheap, and because, once a rotation is forced, a lot of capital may try to find its way into what has become a rather small group of stocks.

Below is a part of a quote I used in my "The Entire Stock Market Hinges On Just 1% of Stocks" article:

In any case, there simply is not enough market capitalization of this relatively handful of companies to go around. Those equities would, in that sense, be overwhelmed, which would be expressed in their share prices. - Horizon Kinetics.

All of this explains why I continue to let energy annoy me, as I believe the opportunities are tremendous. Although this isn't based on exact science, I am convinced there's a path to $90 WTI next year, as supply growth has weakened, cyclical demand could surge substantially, and because the AI trade may shift to "bottleneck investments."

That's also why I like a wide range of energy stocks (to give you some ideas before I end this article):

  • I love high-margin players in America's Permian, including Diamondback Energy (FANG) and Permian Resources (PR). Especially Diamondback is interesting, as it pays variable dividends and has an implied free cash flow yield of 15% at $70 WTI.
  • I love Permian landowners like Texas Pacific Land (TPL), LandBridge (LB), and mineral rights companies like Viper Energy (VNOM).
  • In Canada, I love oil sands operators, including Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) and Suncor Energy (SU). I also love Canadian mineral rights companies like Topaz Energy (TPZ:CA) and PrairieSky Royalty (PSK:CA).
  • Then there are natural gas producers like Antero Resources (AR), Range Resources (RRC), Arc Resources (ARX:CA), and Tourmaline Oil (TOU:CA).
  • I also like coal like Peabody (BTU) and Core Natural Resources (CNR), although these are even more volatile than any of the stocks I just mentioned.
  • I am also starting to like SLB (SLB) and Haliburton (HAL), as we need substantially higher CapEx in the years ahead to support oil supply growth.

Needless to say, there are many other companies.

Takeaway

My main point is that energy could be the best sector to invest in for at least the next decade. While I don't think we should be heavily overweight in energy, but rather stick to diversification, I believe energy will be key to generating alpha for the next few years.

Risks To My Thesis

Energy is super volatile and extremely cyclical. As bullish as I am, a prolonged sideways trend in key leading indicators like the ISM Manufacturing Index could keep pressure on oil. The same goes for new unfavorable supply headlines from OPEC, although I am convinced that these risks are now largely behind us.

Also, stock picking in the energy sector is tricky, as there are countless stocks to pick from, many of which may not deliver the desired returns, even if we are right about the price of oil. In such situations, ETFs may be the best way to go, or "ETF-like" companies like Exxon and Chevron.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase: The Profit Engine That Remains Undervalued
data.description:
Markets surged to fresh record highs on the back of July’s consumer inflation reading. The data increased expectations for a rate cut at the Federal Reserve’s September meeting. Trade sentiment also improved after President Trump extended the U.S.–China tariff truce for an additional 90 days.

While the tone in markets has been broadly optimistic, valuations remain elevated. The S&P 500 trades at valuation levels not seen since the dot-com crash.

Identifying stocks that combine strong fundamentals with cheap valuations should remain a priority for diligent investors. Our AI agent is designed to screen for such opportunities by leveraging proven-superior data to produce more precise, transparent, and reliable signals.

Our Long Idea this week highlights a company with industry-leading profitability, revenue growth across all business segments, record profits, and a cheap stock price.

We originally made JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) a Long Idea in May 2020 and most recently reiterated our position on the stock in November 2024.

JPMorgan Chase recently beat both top- and bottom-line estimates in 2Q25, and now that the company has filed its latest 10-Q, we find that the stock still presents Very Attractive risk/reward.

JPM offers favorable Risk/Reward based on the company’s:

  • Strong growth across both interest income and non-interest income segments,
  • Record high profits,
  • Industry-leading capital ratios and profitability leader, and
  • Cheap stock valuation.

What’s Working

JPMorgan Chase reported strong results across its business in 2Q25. The company’s firmwide average loans and deposits were up 5% and 6% year-over-year (YoY), respectively. More specifically, JPMorgan Chase reported quality growth across all three business segments, including:

  • Debit and credit card sales volume was up 7% YoY in the Consumer & Community banking segment.
  • Investment banking fees were up 7% YoY, and JPMorgan ranked #1 for Global Investment Banking fees with 8.9% wallet share year-to-date (YTD) in its Commercial & Investment Bank segment.
  • Assets under management increased 18% YoY to $4.3 trillion, including $80 billion in net inflows in its Asset & Wealth Management segment.

These results continue a trend of long-term fundamental strength for the largest bank in the United States.

Strong Business Operations and Fundamentals

JPMorgan Chase’s revenue composition reflects a strategically balanced model, with total net revenue split nearly evenly between net interest income [NII] and noninterest revenue [NIR]. This diversification mitigates earnings volatility tied to interest rate fluctuations and reinforces the firm’s resilience across market cycles.

Notably, JPMorgan Chase has demonstrated consistent growth in both revenue streams over the past decade. The company grew NII from $43.6 billion in 2014 to $93.2 billion in the TTM ended 2Q25, which represents compound annual growth of 7%. JPMorgan Chase grew its NIR from $50.6 billion to $82.4 billion over the same time, or 5% compounded annually. See Figure 1.

Figure 1: JPMorgan Chase’s NII and NIR: 2014 – TTM

JPMorgan Chase leverages its diversified business model to continually grow its top and bottom-line.

JPMorgan Chase has grown revenue and net operating profit after tax (NOPAT) by 10% and 11% compounded annually since 2014. See Figure 2. In each of the past three fiscal years, and the TTM ended 2Q25, the company’s NOPAT reached record highs.

The company improved its NOPAT margin from 21% in 2014 to 23% in the TTM, while invested capital turns rose from 0.4 to 0.7 over the same time. Rising NOPAT margins and invested capital turns drive the company’s return on invested capital [ROIC] from 8% in 2014 to 16% in the TTM ended 2Q25.

Additionally, the company’s Core Earnings, a superior and cleaner earnings measure, grew 10% compounded annually from $22.2 billion in 2014 to $60.6 billion in the TTM.

Figure 2: JPMorgan Chase’s Revenue and NOPAT Since 2014

Industry-Leading Profitability

JPMorgan Chase has not only grown revenue and profits to record levels, but it has done so while maintaining industry leading margins and ROIC. As we mentioned earlier in the report, the company has improved both its operational efficiency (NOPAT margin) and its balance sheet efficiency (invested capital turns) over the last decade.

Per Figure 3, JPMorgan Chase has the highest NOPAT margin and ROIC among peers, which include, Goldman Sachs (GS), Citigroup (C), Morgan Stanley (MS), Bank of America (BAC), and Wells Fargo (WFC).

Figure 3: JPMorgan Chase’s Profitability vs. Peers: TTM

Safety That Lasts

Due to its superior capitalization, JPMorgan Chase maintains a stronger financial position than its peers, too.

At the end of 2Q25, JPMorgan Chase’s Common Equity Tier 1 Capital Ratio (CET1), the equity portion of its Tier 1 Capital, was 15.1%, which is up from 12.4% at the end of 2019 and 10.2% in 2014. Importantly, JPMorgan Chase’s CET1 ratio is well above the 4.5% minimum required by the Federal Reserve.

Additionally, the company’s Tier 1 Capital ratio, which measures a bank’s core equity capital to its total risk-weighted assets, is 16.1% through the 2Q25, which is up from 14.1% in 2019 and 11.6% in 2014. Once again, JPMorgan Chase’s Tier 1 Capital ratio is well above the 6.0% minimum required by the Federal Reserve.

JPMorgan Chase’s Tier 1 Capital and CET 1 ratios have been trending higher for over a decade. See Figure 4.

Figure 4: Tier 1 Capital and Common Equity Tier 1 Capital Ratios: 2010 – 2Q25

JPMorgan Chase leads the industry when it comes to capital ratios.

Per Figure 5, JPMorgan Chase’s CET1 ratio ranks above big bank peers mentioned earlier in the report. The company’s Tier 1 Capital ratio is higher than all listed peers except Morgan Stanley.

Figure 5: JPMorgan Chase’s Capital Ratios vs. Peers – 2Q25

Potential for 5%+ Yield

JPMorgan Chase has paid $87.7 billion (11% of market cap) in dividends since 2019 and increased its quarterly dividend from $0.80/share in 2Q19 to $1.40/share in 2Q25. The company’s current dividend, when annualized, provides a 1.9% yield.

JPMorgan Chase also returns capital to shareholders through share repurchases. Since 2019, JPMorgan Chase has repurchased $95.9 billion (12% of market cap) shares. During the first six months of 2025, the company repurchased 15.1 billion shares.

In July 2025, JPMorgan Chase’s Board of Directors authorized a repurchase program of $50 billion. Should the company repurchase shares at the TTM pace, it would repurchase $25.7 billion of shares over the next 12 months, which is 3.2% of the company’s current market cap.

When combined, the dividend and share repurchase yield could reach 5.1%.

Strong Cash Flows Support Shareholder Return

Investors should take comfort in knowing JPMorgan Chase will be able to afford to pay its dividends and repurchase shares due to its large free cash flow (FCF) generation. From 2019 through 1H25, JPMorgan Chase generated $201.2 billion in FCF, which equals 25% of the company’s enterprise value.

Figure 6: JPMorgan Chase’s Cumulative Free Cash Flow: 2019 – 1H25

JPMorgan Chase’s $201.2 billion in FCF since 2019 is more than enough to cover its $183.6 billion in combined dividend payments ($87.7 billion) and share repurchases ($95.9 billion).

JPMorgan Chase’s repurchases have also meaningfully reduced its shares outstanding from 3.1 billion in 2019 to 2.7 billion in 2Q25. See Figure 7.

We like companies that choose to return capital to shareholders instead of spending it on costly acquisitions or executive bonuses that rarely drive shareholder value creation. Companies that sport strong enough cash flows to consistently lower their shares outstanding offer excellent value.

Figure 7: JPMorgan Chase’s Shares Outstanding: 2019 – 2Q25

What’s Not Working

Potential Rate Cuts in the Remainder of 2025

Expectations for a rate cut in September are on the rise, in part due to the latest CPI data and comments from Fed officials. Fed Governor Bowman aims for three interest rate cuts in the remainder of 2025. An interest rate cut would put pressure on all ’banks’—JPMorgan Chase included—net interest margin (NIM) and, in turn, their NII.

In its 2Q25 10-Q, JPMorgan Chase disclosed its earnings-at-risk sensitivity depending on different movements in rates. In this analysis, JPMorgan Chase determined that a 100-basis point interest rate decline results in a $2 billion decrease in earnings. Similarly, a 200-basis point interest rate decline results in a nearly $5 billion decrease in earnings.

The good news? This exposure to lower rates is down from the company’s sensitivity in December 2024, which coincides with rising expectations for a rate cut.

JPMorgan Chase maintains the best capital ratios in the industry and is best positioned to grow noninterest revenue throughout all cycles.

Additionally, any pressure on profits is already more than priced into JPMorgan Chase’s current stock price, as we’ll show below.

Current Price Implies Profits Never Grow Again

At its current price, JPMorgan Chase has a price-to-economic book value (PEBV) ratio of 1.0. This ratio means the market expects the company’s profits to never grow from TTM ended 2Q25 levels. For reference, JPMorgan Chase has grown NOPAT 11% compounded annually over the last five, ten, and 26 (longest period in our model) years.

Below, we use our reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model to quantify the cash flow expectations for different stock price scenarios for JPM.

In the first scenario, we quantify the expectations baked into the current price. If we assume:

  • NOPAT margin immediately falls to 20% (below the five-year average of 26% and TTM NOPAT margin of 23%) through 2034, and
  • Revenue grows 3% compounded annually through 2034 (compared to 13% CAGR over the last five years and 10% CAGR over the last decade), then

The stock would be worth $293/share today—nearly equal to the current stock price. In this scenario, JPMorgan Chase’s NOPAT would grow just 2% compounded annually from 2025–2034, which is well below the company’s historical NOPAT growth rates.

Shares Could Go 20%+ Higher Even If Profits Grow Well Below Historical Rates

If we instead assume:

  • NOPAT margin never improves and is maintained at 23% through 2034, and
  • Revenue grows 5% compounded annually through 2034 (compared to 13% CAGR over the last five years and 10% CAGR over the last decade), then

The stock would be worth $377/share today—a 20%+ upside to the current price. In this scenario, JPMorgan Chase’s NOPAT would grow 5% compounded annually through fiscal 2034, which would still be below the company’s NOPAT compound annual growth rates over the past 5-, 10-, 15-, and 20-year periods.

Should NOPAT grow closer to historical rates, the upside would be even larger.

Figure 8 compares JPMorgan Chase’s historical NOPAT to the NOPAT implied in each of the above scenarios.

Figure 8: JPMorgan Chase’s Historical and Implied NOPAT: DCF Valuation Scenarios

Disclosure: David Trainer owns JPM.

Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Seeking Alpha News Quiz

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the newest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

Wall Street on Friday posted its best five-day advance in over a month, after a host of inflation data further reinforced expectations of a Federal Reserve interest rate cut next week.

On Wednesday, the August producer price index (PPI) unexpectedly slipped on an M/M basis for both core and headline measures. The readings also decelerated on a Y/Y basis from July. Then, on Thursday, the headline August consumer price index (CPI) came in a tad hotter than expected, while the core CPI matched July's rise.

Following recent evidence of cooling in the labor market, the PPI and CPI reports further baked in the odds of interest rate cuts by the Fed this year. The CME FedWatch tool shows traders are all but convinced that the Fed's monetary policy committee will deliver a 25-basis-point rate cut at the conclusion of their meeting next week, followed by two more 25-bps cuts in October and December.

In earnings news, Oracle (ORCL) grabbed the spotlight this week. The cloud software company missed quarterly top and bottom line estimates and kept its forecast unchanged.

But it was another key metric that stole the show: remaining performance obligations (RPO), or the value of committed revenue from signed contracts that has not been recognized as revenue yet. Oracle's (ORCL) RPO more than quadrupled to $455B, with analysts calling it a sign of confidence in the company's capabilities. Shares surged about 36%, adding nearly $250B to the stock's market value and helping Oracle (ORCL) co-founder and chief technology officer Larry Ellison dethrone Elon Musk as the world's richest person.
